name: La Marmota Loop
name_original: Ruta de La Marmota — circular desde La Gineta
description: Jog the difficult 14.0-mile Route of La Marmota — circular from La Gineta, gaining 302 feet over diverse landscapes in 2 hours and 29 minute
region_intro: Jogging routes around La Gineta offer a variety of paths through the region's landscape. The terrain generally features gentle undulations, with some routes incorporating minor elevation gains. This area provides opportunities for both leisurely runs and more challenging long-distance running trails. The network of paths is suitable for exploring the local environment on foot. Best jogging routes around La Gineta The most popular jogging route is La Marmota Loop Trail, a 7.0 miles (11.3 km) trail that takes 1 hour 20 minutes to complete. This circular path offers a moderate workout with some gentle ascents. Another top favourite among local runners is La Marmota Loop, a difficult 13.9 miles (22.5 km) path. This longer route provides a significant challenge with sustained running over varied ground. Local runners also love the Running loop from Casa Bello, a 5.3 miles (8.5 km) trail leading through the local countryside, often completed in about 1 hour 2 minutes. Jogging around La Gineta is defined by open fields and gentle rolling terrain, with a network of routes offering options for easy, moderate, and difficult ability levels. The routes in La Gineta are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.3 stars from 5 reviews. More than 500 runners have used komoot to explore La Gineta's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many running routes can I find in La Gineta?
A: There are over 15 running routes available around La Gineta, offering a variety of distances and difficulty levels for joggers and runners.

Q: Are there easy running routes suitable for beginners in La Gineta?
A: Yes, La Gineta offers several easy running paths. The terrain generally features gentle undulations, making it suitable for those new to running or looking for a relaxed jog. While specific easy routes aren't detailed, the area is known for its accessible paths.

Q: What options are available for long-distance running in La Gineta?
A: For those seeking a significant challenge, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1081126635/la-marmota-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Marmota Loop&lt;/a&gt; is a difficult 22.5 km (13.9 miles) path. This longer route provides sustained running over varied ground, ideal for advanced runners.

Q: Are there any running routes with significant elevation gain in La Gineta?
A: The routes around La Gineta generally feature gentle undulations rather than significant elevation gains. For example, the difficult &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1081126635/la-marmota-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Marmota Loop&lt;/a&gt; has an elevation gain of around 92 meters over its 22.5 km distance, offering a moderate challenge without steep climbs.

Q: Can I find circular running routes in La Gineta?
A: Yes, many of the running paths in La Gineta are circular. A popular option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1081124995/la-marmota-loop-trail" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Marmota Loop Trail&lt;/a&gt;, a 11.3 km (7.0 miles) circular path that offers a moderate workout.

Q: What do other runners say about the trails in La Gineta?
A: The running routes in La Gineta are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.3 stars from 5 reviews. Over 500 runners have used komoot to explore the varied terrain, often praising the open fields and gentle rolling landscape.

Q: Are there any interesting landmarks or attractions along the running routes?
A: While running, you might encounter points of interest such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1399790" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Acequión Aqueduct&lt;/a&gt; or the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/3104463" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Marmota Fire Lookout Tower&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5837487" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Natural Trail of the María Cristina Canal&lt;/a&gt; also offers scenic sections.

Q: Are the running trails in La Gineta suitable for families?
A: The gentle rolling terrain and network of paths in La Gineta make it suitable for family outings. Many routes are moderate in difficulty, offering an enjoyable experience for various fitness levels. Consider shorter, less challenging loops for younger family members.

Q: Is there public transport access to the running routes in La Gineta?
A: La Gineta is a rural area, and public transport options directly to trailheads might be limited. It's generally recommended to travel by car to access the starting points of the running routes. For specific routes, check local transport schedules or consider starting from the town center.

Q: Where can I park when visiting the running trails in La Gineta?
A: Parking availability varies by trailhead. Many routes start from accessible points near the town or rural roads where roadside parking may be possible. For routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20130293" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Casa Bello&lt;/a&gt;, you might find parking near local establishments or designated areas.

Q: What is a good running route to explore the local countryside?
A: The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/20130293" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Running loop from Casa Bello&lt;/a&gt; is an excellent choice. This 8.5 km (5.3 miles) trail leads through the local countryside, offering a pleasant experience of the region's natural beauty.

Q: Are there any hidden gem running routes in La Gineta?
A: While not widely publicized, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/38171548" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;La Marmota Fire Lookout Tower loop from Casa de las Viñas&lt;/a&gt; is a shorter, 5.1 km route that offers a unique perspective and a chance to discover a less-trafficked path to a local landmark.
tips:
The church of San Martín de Tours is the most important and significant building in the town, which also has good mansions, with bars and balconies of artistic value. As in most of the towns in the region, the church tower marks the most characteristic point of the Manchego landscape.
With a height of more than 46 meters that make it visible from 10 kilometers in all directions, it is a magnificent masonry tower very different from others in the region, topped with a Renaissance lantern with decorative Italianate-inspired buttresses and crowned by a weathervane of wrought iron. Its interior is crossed by a steep spiral staircase that reaches the bells.
The church, late Gothic, with Renaissance elements from the 16th century, with a single nave with striking helicoidal pillars and complicated ribbed vaults. The main cover, the work of Jerónimo Quijano. Other critics attribute it to the great architect Andrés de Vandelvira. It is in the Renaissance style, very simple, with straight lines, with semi-recessed Doric columns.

On the Ruta de la Marmota in the Sierra de Alcaraz in Albacete, there is a forest watchtower that is located on top of Los Villares peak, which can be seen from various points on the route. It is used for the prevention and detection of forest fires in the area.
The Camino de Casa Quemada a Los Pontones is one of the most outstanding sections of the La Marmota route, as it offers impressive views of the mountainous landscapes and the local fauna and flora.

faqs.question: Where does the Route of La Marmota — circular from La Gineta start and where can I park?
faqs.answer: The route is circular from La Gineta. While specific parking areas aren't detailed, La Gineta is the starting and ending point, suggesting parking would be available within or on the outskirts of the town. Look for public parking options in La Gineta.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete this jogging route?
faqs.answer: The Route of La Marmota is approximately 22.5 km (14 miles) long. For jogging, it typically takes around 2 hours and 30 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and any stops you make.

faqs.question: What is the terrain like on this jogging route?
faqs.answer: The terrain primarily consists of an earth path and a general trail, with some rocky sections. It follows traditional cattle tracks, including the Cañada Real de Pozo Rubio. The paths are mostly accessible, making it suitable for jogging.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect along the way?
faqs.answer: You can expect a diverse landscape, including pine forests (Pinares de Pozo Rubio), cultivated fields, and riparian forest near the Júcar River. A particularly scenic section is the path from Camino de Casa Quemada to Los Pontones, offering impressive views of mountainous landscapes and local flora.

faqs.question: What notable landmarks or points of interest are on the route?
faqs.answer: Key landmarks include the imposing Church of San Martín in La Gineta, whose tower is visible from a distance. You'll also pass by the La Marmota Fire Lookout Tower on Pico Los Villares, which can be seen from various points along the route.

faqs.question: What wildlife might I encounter on the trail?
faqs.answer: The area is ecologically rich. Keep an eye out for signs of various mammals such as foxes, pine martens, badgers, Eurasian otters, wild boars, red squirrels, and rabbits. The route also features specific signposts related to local plant species.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to jog the Route of La Marmota?
faqs.answer: The trail is generally accessible, but for jogging, spring and autumn offer the most pleasant temperatures. Summers can be hot, and winters might be chilly, so plan accordingly for weather conditions.

faqs.question: Is the Route of La Marmota — circular from La Gineta dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route is generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially when passing through cultivated fields or near wildlife habitats, and to carry water for both yourself and your pet.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no permits required or entrance fees to jog the Route of La Marmota — circular from La Gineta. It is a publicly accessible trail.

faqs.question: Does the trail intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other paths. Notably, it covers sections of the Camino de Santiago del Sureste, Camino de Santiago de Levante, and the Camino de Casa Quemada a Los Pontones, among others like the Cañada Real de Pozo Rubio.

faqs.question: Are there any cafes, pubs, or accommodation options in La Gineta?
faqs.answer: As the starting and ending point, La Gineta is a town where you can find local amenities such as cafes, pubs, and accommodation options to refresh yourself before or after your jogging adventure.
